coredata.dc:title: Highly selective and sensitive fluorescence sensing of nanomolar Zn2+ ions in aqueous medium using Calix[4]arene passivated Carbon Quantum Dots based on fluorescence enhancement: Real-time monitoring and intracellular investigation
coredata.dc:creator: Kaur, Harpreet
Raj, Pushap
Sharma, Hemant
Verma, Meenakshi
Singh, Narinder
Kaur, Navneet
coredata.dc:description: Carbon Quantum Dots (CDs) owing to its unique photophysical properties have attracted immense consideration of the researchers in last decade to explore its applications in the fields of bio-imaging and sensing. In the present work, we have developed a novel CDs functionalized with Calix[4]arene derivative by hydrothermal route to detect biologically pertinent Zn2+ ions. Surface modification of CDs enhances fluorescence efficiency apart from introducing active surface sites broadening its sensor capabilities. The obtained surface-functionalized CDs exhibits a strong blue fluorescence under UV light (365 nm) with a quantum yield (Φ) of 56% which is appreciably good. Zn2+ ions get apprehended in the cavity formed due to passivation by Calix[4]arene moeity on the exterior of CDs having profuse –NH2 groups. Addition of Zn2+ ions into the solution of CDs results in remarkable enhancement in the fluorescence intensity attributed to Photo-induced Electron Transfer (PET) “OFF” phenomenon. The CDs based nanosensor exhibited a wide linear response with a detection limit of 7.34 nM. Significantly, CDs were shown to display insignificant cytotoxicity, decent biocompatibility, and virtuous resistance to photo-bleaching, good stability over a wide range of pH, insignificant variation in the emission intensity in the presence of high concentration of salt and excellent bio-labeling ability when tested on E. coli cell lines. Additionally, the detection results of Zn2+ ions obtained in real samples were in complete agreement with that obtained by Atomic Absorption Spectroscopy (AAS), signifying the prospective application of the proposed probe as a selective and sensitive sensor for Zn2+ ions. The proposed sensor was also applied for fluorescence imaging of intracellular Zn2+ ions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots (CDs)
Calix [4] arene
Sensor
Surface functionalization
Fluorescence spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Surface modification of CDs and characterization
Fluorescent nano probe for Zn2+ ions determination
Synthesis and spectral characterization of CDs
Fluorescence response in the presence of metal ions

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India
Council of Scientific and Industrial Research, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Research grants from DST-INSPIRE and CSIR Project 02(0216)/14/EMR-II is highly acknowledged by the authors.
Research grants from DST-INSPIRE and CSIR Project 02(0216)/14/EMR-II is highly acknowledged by the authors.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structure elucidation of (a) CDs, (b) 1,3-disubstituted Calix[4]arene molecule, and (c) Surface functionalized CDs; DLS histogram of (d) CDs, and (e) Surface functionalized CDs depicting size of the order of 15 nm and 36 nm, respectively; (f) Representation of surface functionalized CDs under UV Light (λ = 365 nm); (g) HR-TEM of surface functionalized CDs; (h) FT-IR Spectrum of CDs, Surface functionalized CDs and Calix[4]arene molecule.

(a) UV-Visible absorption spectrum, and (b) Excitation-dependent Emission Spectrum (λex = 330 nm–415 nm) of surface functionalized CDs; (c) Fluorescence emission graph of normalized intensity showing red shift in wavelength upon varying λmax; (d) Fluorescence Emission Spectrum showing quenching in intensity upon increasing water content due to self-assembly of CDs in water. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) Emission spectrum of CDs in different concentrations of water showing aqueous stability up to 36 h; (b) Fluorescence profile at different intervals of time when irradiated in UV light (365 nm) showing excellent resistance to photo-bleaching. INSET: Visual changes of color with increasing water percentage as seen under day-light; (c) Fluorescence profile showing effect of pH on surface functionalized CDs and CDs treated with Zn2+(40 μM) at varying pH; (d) Pictorial representation of Polymer-based thin film containing (above) sensor assembly, and (below) sensor assembly having Zn2+ ions. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra showing selectivity of calix-modified CDs-based sensor assembly for Zn2+ ions over other metal ions at pH 7.0 in 20 mM Tris-HCl buffer; (b) FL response of CDs (λex = 330 nm) upon addition of increasing concentration of Zn2+ ions in DMSO: Water (2: 1, v/v) at pH 7.0, INSET: Calibration plot displaying linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and Zn2+ concentration, and (c) Relative fluorescence intensities of CDs in DMSO: Water (2: 1, v/v) with different competitive metal ions (40 μM) in the presence of Zn2+ ions (40 μM); (d) Fluorescence response of Zn2+ at varying concentration (0–40 μM) at different intervals of time in DMSO: Water (2: 1, v/v).

Fluorescence microscope images of (A) E. coli cells after incubation with functionalized CDs for 24 h at 37 °C, (B) E. coli cells-control experiment, and (C) E. coli cells labeled with functionalized CDs in the presence of Zn2+ ions with an excitation wavelength of 330 nm.
The fluorescence response of few reported probes selective for Zn2+ ions in different solvent system.

head.ce:title: Highly selective and sensitive fluorescence sensing of nanomolar Zn2+ ions in aqueous medium using Calix[4]arene passivated Carbon Quantum Dots based on fluorescence enhancement: Real-time monitoring and intracellular investigation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre for Nanoscience and Nanotechnology, Panjab University, Chandigarh, 160014, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, IIT Ropar, Roopnagar, Punjab, 140001, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Panjab University, Chandigarh, 160014, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon Quantum Dots (CDs) owing to its unique photophysical properties have attracted immense consideration of the researchers in last decade to explore its applications in the fields of bio-imaging and sensing. In the present work, we have developed a novel CDs functionalized with Calix[4]arene derivative by hydrothermal route to detect biologically pertinent Zn2+ ions. Surface modification of CDs enhances fluorescence efficiency apart from introducing active surface sites broadening its sensor capabilities. The obtained surface-functionalized CDs exhibits a strong blue fluorescence under UV light (365 nm) with a quantum yield (Φ) of 56% which is appreciably good. Zn2+ ions get apprehended in the cavity formed due to passivation by Calix[4]arene moeity on the exterior of CDs having profuse –NH2 groups. Addition of Zn2+ ions into the solution of CDs results in remarkable enhancement in the fluorescence intensity attributed to Photo-induced Electron Transfer (PET) “OFF” phenomenon. The CDs based nanosensor exhibited a wide linear response with a detection limit of 7.34 nM. Significantly, CDs were shown to display insignificant cytotoxicity, decent biocompatibility, and virtuous resistance to photo-bleaching, good stability over a wide range of pH, insignificant variation in the emission intensity in the presence of high concentration of salt and excellent bio-labeling ability when tested on E. coli cell lines. Additionally, the detection results of Zn2+ ions obtained in real samples were in complete agreement with that obtained by Atomic Absorption Spectroscopy (AAS), signifying the prospective application of the proposed probe as a selective and sensitive sensor for Zn2+ ions. The proposed sensor was also applied for fluorescence imaging of intracellular Zn2+ ions.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CDs) are fluorescent carbon spheres having size less than 10 nm. Recently, CDs are receiving considerable attention owing to their abundant features and fascinating properties. On account of their solubility and fluorescence, these are also referred to as carbon nano-lights [1]. An assortment of methods have been developed for the synthesis of CDs namely, laser ablation, arc discharge, solvothermal/hydrothermal carbonization, microwave irradiation and electrochemical oxidation [2–5]. All these methods were established to improve the functionality as well as optical properties of the CDs. However, these methods exhibits certain disadvantages in terms of, expensive precursors, convoluted processes, harsh synthetic conditions and relatively low quantum yield, which sternly limits the availability of large quantities of CDs for practical applications. Hence, it is required to develop a more simplistic, eco-friendly and inexpensive approach to fabricate CDs at large scale.

The eminent properties of CDs such as outstanding electron donating/accepting capability, high solubility, biocompatibility, low toxicity, robust chemical inertness, facile modification, resistance to photo-bleaching and excellent fluorescent properties (broad excitation spectrum and narrow and tunable emission spectrum) entrust them as a good candidate for a diverse range of applications for instance, optoelectronics, biomolecule/drug delivery, sensing, bio imaging, photo catalysis and bioelectronics [6–8]. In literature, CDs have been synthesized from glycerol, glycol and various other saccharides in the presence of inorganic ion [9]. Another group has demonstrated the formation of monodisperse CDs via microwave assisted method by irradiating glucose in the presence of poly(ethylene glycol) [10].

Fine-tuning the chemical structure of the core as well as surface of the CDs could endow them with potential to be applied in innumerable applications. In literature, there are countless approaches to functionalize the surface of CDs via surface chemistry and different interactions such as covalent bonding, coordination,π-π interactions, and sol-gel technology [11,12]. Most of the CDs contain oxygen-containing functional groups on their surface, which apart from refining their solubility, also endows them with feasibility in covalent bonding. Modification/passivation and doping the surface with nitrogen-containing functional groups expands the properties of the CDs by recuperating the optical, chemical and electrical properties [13]. Surface functionalization alters the surface states and provides CDs a number of functionalities widening their applications.

Herein, we report a facile preparative route for the synthesis of CDs having profuse free –NH2 groups on the surface via hydrothermal carbonization of glycerol and ethylene diamine [14]. The as-synthesized CDs were then surface modified using 1,3-disubstituted calix[4]arene molecule. This supramolecule was consciously chosen to design a CD-based assembly having plentiful rigid cavities of unalterable sizes so as to embrace metal ion of particular size. The spectroscopic properties of the surface functionalized CDs were then investigated in detail; which demonstrates that this surface functionalized CDs could serve as a very effectual fluorescent sensing platform for selective and sensitive detection of metal ions Although, a number of CD-based fluorescent probes have been developed for the detection of metal ions; however, they do not exhibit any evident advantage over other fluorescent probes based on organic molecules and nanomaterial. Therefore, it is desirable to design an efficient CD-based fluorescent sensing system bearing well-functionalized surface and showing remarkable fluorescent properties [15].

Hence, novel calix[4]arene-based surface passivated CDs were developed as a potential sensor for Zn2+ ions in semi-aqueous medium which is the second most abundant transition metal ion and an essential ion in human body [16]. Zn2+ ions are known to play a crucial role in various biological processes, for instance, cellular metabolism, antioxidant enzyme activity, gene transcription, DNA structural integrity, oocytes maturation, fertilization, brain function and immune function [17–20]. The permissible dietary intake of Zn2+ ions is 8–11 mg/day [20]. Excess of this metal ion in human body subjugates the uptake of other essential metal ions and is also identified as a cause in serious disorders such as diabetes, cerebral ischemia, epilepsy and Alzheimer's disease, cancer, neural degeneration and also leads to cytotoxicity [21]. Also, deficiency of the same can cause diarrhea, reduces childhood growth, abnormal brain development and other immune deficiencies [22]. In many parts of the world, diarrhea is the primary cause of mortality among infants and children below the age of 5 years in developing countries [23]. Diarrhea was accountable for the 13% of the deaths among young children all the years as per recent findings in literature [24]. Hence, two simple and effective methods are proposed to treat diarrhea in acute cases, which includes using low concentrations of oral rehydration salts (ORS) and providing zinc supplementation (10–20 mg/day) [25]. Deficiency of zinc is expressly common in developing nations because of low dietary intake of zinc-rich food and poor absorption of the cation due to its binding to dietary fibers [25]. Zinc supplementation and zinc fortification/bio-fortification are expected to increase crop yield and provide more dietary zinc. Supplementing zinc in any form reduces the severity, duration and reoccurrence of diarrhea, though the mechanism of its action and anti-diarrheal effect is not yet understood [26]. Also, wheat and maize flour-fortification has recently come into picture in order to provide adequate zinc to effected population globally.

However, diagnosing deficiency of zinc is still a challenging field as there are not many reports in literature which shows the detection of the metal ion at nano-molar concentration accurately. The reported fluorescent-based sensors display a number of drawbacks in terms of complex synthesis procedures, fluorescence quenching and cross-sensitivities from various competitive metal ions. Zn2+ ions due to the fundamental d10 electronic configuration pose interference from other metal ions such as Cd2+ and Hg2+ in designing sensors with high selectivity, sensitivity and reliability. Hence, development of competent fluorescent sensor able to detect Zn2+ ions in highly competitive medium is pivotal. Despite of the innumerable reported sensors for the Zn2+ ions, there is a need to design proficient sensors having enhanced selectivity and sensitivity with low detection limit. The intensiometric probe thus developed based on the mechanism of Photo-induced Electron Transfer (PET) abolishes these problems and was able to detect Zn2+ ions selectively with a detection limit at nano-molar level [27].

Also, in order to diversify the applicability of the sensor assembly, the bio-imaging experiments were also carried out and the as-synthesized sensor assembly showed excellent bio-labeling capability. The sensor assembly was also applied for real-time detection of Zn2+ ions in commercially available ORS and zinc supplements (tablet) showing accurate results as compared to those obtained using standard Atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) method. With each application elaborated in this article, the prepared CDs unlock new horizons for quantitative detection, bio-imaging and real-time monitoring of the target Zn2+ions.

Glycerol, ethylene diamine, p-tert butyl phenol, formaldehyde (37%), NaOH, K2CO3, 1,2-dibromoethane and p-hydroxy benzaldehyde were procured from Sigma-Aldrich and were used without further purification. All the metal nitrate salts were procured from SD Fine and the aqueous solutions of the same were prepared in Type-I water obtained from Millipore water purification system. Solvents such as diphenyl ether, toluene, acetone, acetonitrile and DMSO were obtained from SD Fine Chemicals. Deionised water (Type-I) was used throughout the experiments.

CDs were synthesized using hydrothermal carbonization which is a low cost, non-toxic and eco-friendly route to obtain CDs from different precursors. Typically, in this method, a solution of organic precursor (carbon-yielding compound) in suitable solvent (high boiling) is sealed and reacted in a hydrothermal reactor at high temperature, followed by extraction and concentration. We have employed a reported method for the fabrication of CDs from glycerol and ethylene diamine in water as a solvent [14]. Glycerol (136 mmol) was dissolved in 12 ml water to obtain solution (I) and ethylenediamine (149 mmol) was dissolved in 6 ml of distilled water to obtain another solution (II). 4 ml of solution I was taken and mixed with solution II and sonicated for about 15 min at 25±2 °C. The mixture was then transferred to Teflon reaction vessel and was then sealed. The autoclave containing the vessel was then kept in oven at 180 °C for 12 h to obtain fluorescent CDs. After heating, the autoclave was allowed to cool at room temperature on a heat-resistant plate. The solution was then filtered using a 0.22 μm membrane to confiscate any unreacted organic molecules left in the reaction mixture followed by washing with dichloromethane. The CDs thus obtained were viewed under UV irradiation of 365 nm and it appeared to show blue fluorescence (Fig. 1 F). The material was then characterized using FT-IR, NMR, DLS, and UV-Visible absorption and fluorescence emission spectroscopy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface modification of CDs and characterization
ce:section.ce:para: Surface modification is carried out to enhance the fluorescence properties of the CDs. Surface functionalization of the as-synthesized CDs was done with calix[4]arene derivative. 1,3-disubstituted calix[4]arene was synthesized by literature method [25] and was then dissolved in DMSO (0.5 mmol). The solution was refluxed for half an hour to dissolve the organic molecule and CDs were then added to it and the reaction mixture was then stirred at room temperature for 4 h. The material was characterized using FT-IR during the course of reaction every half an hour to monitor the substitution at the surface of the CDs. At the culmination of the reaction when whole of the calixarene molecule got consumed and no peak for –CHO group in the IR spectrum was observed, the stirring was discontinued. The material was then freeze dried and purified using centrifugation followed by dialysis. Functionalized CDs were then characterized using FT-IR, NMR, TEM and DLS confirming the formation of optimum-sized, mono-dispersed surface functionalized CDs in semi-aqueous medium.

The photophysical properties of the surface functionalized CDs were then investigated using UV-Visible absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy. The UV-Visible absorption profile shows absorption peak in the UV region and emission profile of the CDs showed typical excitation-wavelength-dependent fluorescence emission in the blue region of the electromagnetic spectrum. Effect of pH, water content and salt concentration was also explored, for which the material was subjected to acid-base titration, increasing concentration of water and high concentration of perchlorate salt. Photo-stability is another important feature of the CDs for biological application, for which the material was exposed to UV irradiation for a time span of 120 min and fluorescence spectrum was noted throughout the irradiation duration at fixed interval of time. With the purpose of studying the stability of the surface functionalized CDs at varying pH, fluorescence emission of surface functionalized CDs were noted at pH ranging from 2 to 14 using diluted solutions of HCl (0.1 M) and NaOH (0.1 M). In order to study the photo stability of the as-prepared CDs, the solution of CDs was irradiated under UV light of 365 nm and fluorescence emission spectrum were noted at a fixed interval of irradiation for a duration of 2 h. To study the effect of high concentration of salt, gradual aliquots of tetrabutylammonium perchlorate salt (0–320 μM) was added to CDs and fluorescence emission was noted. Aqueous stability of the surface functionalized CDs was then investigated, for which CDs were subject to two different ratios of DMSO and water (2:1 and 1:10). Fluorescence emission spectra were noted at varying interval of time for a period of 36 h and plot of fluorescence emission with respect to time was made.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent nano probe for Zn2+ ions determination
ce:section.ce:para: In order to execute the fluorescence experimentation, excitation was given at 330 nm and the emission was examined at 418 nm. The volume of each sample taken was 3 mL in 20 mM Tris-HCl buffer and all the experiments were performed at 25±2 °C. Using the above mention buffer solution, pH of the solutions were set at 7.0 and addition of micro molar quantities of metal ions during binding studies, did not altered the pH value. Standard aqueous solutions of nitrate salts of library of metal ions namely, Na+, K+, Li2+, Cs2+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Hg2+, Ag+ and Al3+ were prepared using de-ionized water having concentration of 5 mM. For metal binding studies, 40 μM of these standard solutions were added to 3 ml of surface functionalized CDs taken in volumetric flasks. The solutions were well-shaken and kept for at least half an hour before noting their fluorescence spectrum. Forthe titration experiment, the CDs (3 ml) taken in volumetric flask was subjected to increasing concentrations of Zn2+ ions (0–40 μM) and fluorescence emission was scrutinized. For interference studies, the CDs containing 40 μM of Zn2+ ions in separate volumetric flasks were subjected to potentially interfering cations, namely, Na+, K+, Li2+, Cs2+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Hg2+, Ag+ and Al3+ (40 μM) and fluorescence emission spectra were noted after allowing the samples to rest for at least half an hour after proper trembling. Further, response time of the binding process was also evaluated for which, five different concentrations of Zn2+ ions were added to CDs (0–40 μM) and fluorescence intensity profile was plotted with respect to time (in seconds).

The quantum yield of the CDs was investigated using 9, 10-diphenylanthracene as standard having quantum yield of 0.88 as reported in literature [28]. In order to abate the inner filter effect and re-absorption effects, the absorbance of the standard sample as well as the CDs was kept below 0.10. For the experimentation part, five different concentrations of each compound were made. The reference material was dissolved in ethanol and the CDs were dissolved in water and the fluorescence spectra were noted at same excitation wavelength. Then the quantum yield was calculated by comparing the integrated fluorescence intensities and absorbance values using the following equation:
Φs = Φr [Grad s/Grad r] [ɳs 2/ɳr 2]
Where, subscripts ‘s’ and ‘r’ refers to sample and reference/standard material; Φ denotes quantum yield; Grad represents gradient value from the plot of integrated fluorescence intensity vs. absorbance and ɳ is the refractive index of the solvent employed [29]. The quantum yield obtained was of the order of 56% which is appreciably high.

Carbon quantum dots (CDs) owing to its fascinating property of tunable surface modification, excellent dispersibility in water, aqueous stability, low cytotoxicity, biocompatibility and good optical performance makes them a suitable candidate for applications such as fluorescence sensing and bio-imaging [30,31]. However, exploration of CDs in the field of fluorescent sensing and biological applications still remain at an early stage of research. Under present investigation, we attempted to functionalize 1,3-disubstitued calix[4]arene molecule over the surface of CDs. We purposely chose this supramolecule because of its captivating properties such as pre-organized 3D structure having hydrophobic cavity, tunable cavity size, and ease of functionalization [32]. Surface functionalization/modification with different organic/inorganic molecules, polymers, metals etc. is well reported in literature as this is an effectual methodology for modifying the properties of CDs [33,34]. CDs without modification at their surface showed constrained dispersibility, low fluorescent quantum yield and low solubility limiting their applications [35,36]. Surface functionalization alters the excitation/emission profile of the CDs by introducing different emissive surface states which may coalesce with the intrinsic band gap core via electron/energy transfer [37]. Surface modification is mostly studied to enhance the emission of the CDs caused by an upward shift in the Fermi level and electrons in the conduction band [38]. The surface functionalized CDs exhibit excellent photo-reversibility, photo stability and chemical stability [39]. Furthermore, increased fluorescence quantum yield is observed after passivation by covalent bonding or organic functional groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and spectral characterization of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Herein, we report the fabrication of CD-based fluorescent sensor for Zn2+ ions in semi-aqueous medium. CDs having abundant functional groups on the surface were obtained from glycerol and ethylene diamine in aqueous medium using hydrothermal reaction procedure carried out in Teflon-lined autoclave for 12 h at 180 °C. The material was characterized using FTIR spectroscopy and it showed peaks at 3317 cm−1 and 1597 cm−1 which are attributed to asymmetric stretching deformation vibrations of –NH2 implying presence of abundant NH2 groups on the surface of CDs. Distinct peaks at 1234 cm−1, 1288 cm−1 and 1381 cm−1 may be credited to C=C stretching of the aromatic rings. Another peak at 926 cm−1 was ascribed to the out-of-plane bending vibrations of = CH groups of the aromatic rings (Fig. 1H). Apart from FT-IR spectroscopy, the carbon dots (CDs) were characterized using 1H NMR technique in DMSO-d6 using trimethylsilane (TMS) as reference. 1H NMR (400 MHz, DMSO-d6): 3.23 (d, CH3), 3.26 (d, CH3), 3.34 (d, CH2), 3.39 (m, CH2) and 4.30 (s, NH2) as shown in Fig. S1.

For the fabrication of the sensing assembly, calix[4]arene derivative was reacted with CDs in DMSO and the progress of coating was monitored through IR spectroscopy every half an hour during the course of reaction. At the commencement of the reaction, discernible bands at 3363 cm−1 (OH stretching), 1010 cm−1 (C-O stretching), 945 cm−1 (C-O-C stretching), 1651 cm−1, 2908 cm−1 (weak) and 2893 cm−1 (weak) due to –CHO group of the calix[4]arene derivative were present, which got diminished as the reaction progressed. The band at 1651 cm−1 frequency is due to C=O stretching and two weak bands at 2908 and 2893 cm−1 are attributable to (CO)-H stretching. As the reaction concluded, new bands at 1644 cm−1 were observed for the imine group formed by the substitution reaction at the surface of CDs. The final product thus obtained was freeze dried followed by centrifugation and dialysis and then the FTIR spectrum was noted, which showed decrease in the intensity of the peak at ∼3400 cm−1 indicating consumption of the –NH2 groups and confirmed the formation of a covalent bond between calix[4]arene moiety and –NH2 rich surface of the CDs. Also, a discernible peak at 1644 cm−1 was observed which could be ascribed to stretching frequency of imine bond formed as shown in Fig. 1H. In addition to FT-IR spectroscopy, the surface functionalized carbon dots were characterized using 1H NMR technique in DMSO-d6 using trimethylsilane (TMS) as reference. 1H NMR (400 MHz, DMSO-d6): 1.08 (s, CH3), 1.14 (s, CH3), 3.12 (s, CH3), 3.18 (s, CH3), 3.22 (s, CH2), 3.47 (s, CH2), 8.27 (s, CH=N) and a few signals in the aromatic region which could be accounted to Calix[4]arene moiety (Fig. S2).

Apart from NMR and IR spectroscopy, the final product was further characterized using techniques such as TEM and DLS which showed that CDs are well dispersed in solution phase and exhibit an average size of 15 nm in unmodified state and 36 nm for surface functionalized CDs as depicted in Fig. 1D, E and 1G. The optical properties of the CDs are dependent on the defective state emission and quantum confinement. The coexistence of these defect sites and an assembly of sp2 carbon could proficiently increase the band gap giving rise to stronger visible fluorescence [40]. An unmodified surface of CDs have innumerable defective sites, for example, swaying bonds, non-radiative states and free radicals making CDs susceptible to various contaminants that may lead to photo-bleaching and low quantum yields. Surface passivation is therefore done to enhance the photo stability and quantum yield of CDs.

The photophysical property of the CDs and surface functionalized CDs were then appraised using UV-Visible absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy. CDs are known to possess tunable excitation and emission spectra, therefore, an appropriate excitation wavelength could be chosen. CDs may exhibit σ-σ*, σ-π*, π-π* and n-π* transitions owing to its chemical structure [41]. Presence of functional groups bearing lone pair of electrons bonded to the aromatic sp2 hybridized carbon, can lead to electronic transitions from the n-states of the functional group present to the π*-states of the aromatic carbon. Therefore, CDs are expected to show absorption in the UV region with a tail normally extended to the visible region. Such an absorption spectrum is attributed to π-π* transitions of the C=C bonds and n- π* transitions of C=O bonds [37].

Fluorescence of CDs is the most enthralling feature, proportionally for fundamental as well as application perspective. From the literature, it is quite evident that fluorescence intensity of CDs is excitation-wavelength-dependent phenomenon owing to the quantum effect, various emissive sites on the surface of CDs and presence of different polyaromatic fluorophores within the carbogenic core [42]. It is worth mentioning here that the fluorescence range of most of the CDs synthesized till date mainly covers the blue region of the electromagnetic spectrum corresponding to wavelengths from 400 to 500 nm.

The absorption profile of the surface functionalized CDs (Fig. 2 A) shows a peak at 270 nm accountable to π-π* transitions and another shoulder peak at 290 nm which may be attributed to n-π* transitions of C=O bond [34–36]. The CDs exhibits bright blue colored fluorescence (Fig. 1F) and shows excitation-wavelength-dependent behavior when the excitation wavelength is larger than 300 nm (Fig. 2B). A stokes shift of 88 nm was observed with surface functionalized CDs which could be assigned to splitting of the exciton states by electron-hole exchange interaction via de-excitation mechanism. The strong emission profile of the CDs could be assigned to sharp energy traps in the CDs that gets stabilized and give high fluorescence. The optical properties of the CDs were further scrutinized, for which the fluorescence emission profile was observed at progressively increasing excitation wavelength as shown in Fig. 2B. In the emission profile of surface functionalized CDs, excitation-wavelength-dependent fluorescence properties were observed as consonant with CDs. In the fluorescence spectrum, with increasing excitation wavelength, a red shift from 418 nm to 467 nm was observed accompanied by a diminution in the fluorescence intensity which may be ascribed to the fact that fluorescence of the CDs is contingent with the excitation wavelength.

A predominant feature of the bare and surface functionalized CDs is the solvent-dependent fluorescence intensity. In order to understand the effect of water on the stability of CDs, the former were mixed with water in increasing concentrations and emission profile was investigated. As could be perceived from Fig. 2D, as the concentration of water was increased the emission intensity was found to drop which could be accountable to the self-assembly of surface functionalized CDs in presence of water [36]. The aqueous stability of the CDs was also investigated by observing the fluorescence profile of the same over a period of time and it was shown to exhibit a good aqueous stability for 36 h as shown in Fig. 3 A. It is a well-known fact that photo-stability of the CDs plays an imperative role in the fluorescence detection [37]. In association with this, we investigated the emission profile of the surface functionalized CDs when given an UV irradiation of 365 nm for 120 min as shown in Fig. 3B above. It was observed that fluorescence intensity remained stable even after 120 min of irradiation and no observable photo-bleaching was witnessed, authenticating good photo-stability of the synthesized CDs. With the purpose of investigating the selectivity, sensitivity and precision of the sensor based on CDs, various physical parameters including the medium pH, high salt concentration, and water content were needed to be monitored. For this, the proposed sensor was subjected to high concentration of perchlorate salt as it was shown to display negligible change in the fluorescence profile as could be seen in Fig. S3. Also, on varying the pH of the solution from acidic to basic, it could be well established that our sensor works best in the pH range of 4–12 (Fig. 3C). However, at a pH lower than 4, fluorescence profile was observed to change drastically and pH above 12 showed quenching in the fluorescence intensity suggesting strong dependence of the fluorescence intensity of CDs on the pH value of the system. This variation in the pH may be assigned to ionization of the –OH groups of calix[4]arene derivative functionalized on the surface of CDs which is expected to affect the confinement energy of CDs resulting in the change in fluorescence. Our results are consistent with the literature findings of CDs functionalized with hydroxyl and carboxylic/carbonyl moieties. Consequently, we selected 7.0 as the optimal pH and carried out the studies at this pH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response in the presence of metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: Under the same experimental conditions, the surface functionalized CDs were then explored for its sensor capabilities. For fluorescence studies, excitation was given at 330 nm and the emission was observed at 418 nm. The volume of each sample taken was 3 ml in 20 mM Tris-HCl buffer (pH 7.0). All the experiments were carried out at 25 ± 2 °C. The solution in the volumetric flasks were subjected to 40 μM of nitrate salts of various cations namely, Na+, K+, Li2+, Cs2+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Hg2+, Ag+ and Al3+, and it was shown to exhibit remarkable enhancement in the fluorescence intensity with Zn2+ and negligible change with other cationic solutions employed (Fig. 4 A). The enhancement in the fluorescence intensity on addition of Zn2+ ions, may be attributed to PET (Photo-induced electron transfer) “OFF” phenomenon. PET process generally occurs upon excitation of electrons from the nitrogen donor atoms to the π-system of the receptor unit [49]. Zn2+ is photophysically inactive due to closed shell d-orbitals and cannot induce electron or energy transfer with the receptor molecule [50]. As a result of which zinc ions are expected to effect the fluorescence emission of the receptor by preventing the electron transfer leading to enhancement or regeneration of the fluorescence emission of the receptor molecule [51–53]. Zn2+ ions were embraced in the cavity of the sensor assembly, thereby engaging the lone pairs of the –OH groups and sp2 N of the imine groups restraining the electron donating abilities of the two groups, consequently switching ‘OFF’ the PET phenomenon. The suppression in the intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) and induced rigidity in the receptor molecule upon binding with Zn2+ ions leads to change in the fluorescent properties of the receptor [54]. The sp2 N of the imine linkage and the hydroxyl groups were designed to bond with Zn2+ ions as it is well known that schiff's bases with π electrons in the imine group provides excellent possibility to chelate with metal ions [55]. When complex is formed, intramolecular charge transfer is suppressed by the electron withdrawing property of the Zn2+ ions leading to enhancement in the fluorescence emission accountable to PET “OFF” mechanism.

Further, titration experiment was carried out to substantiate the binding process, for which, CDs were subjected to increasing concentration of Zn2+ ions from 0 to 40 μM. Fig. 4B shows the fluorescence intensity of CDs in the presence of different concentrations of Zn2+ ions in Tris-HCl buffer solution. The fluorescence intensity could be seen increasing as the concentration of Zn2+ ions was increased and it displayed good linearity in binding with a linear regression coefficient of 0.99079 as shown in the inset of Fig. 4B below. The detection limit of the proposed system was evaluated to be 7.34 × 10−9 M using 3σ method [43]. All the above observations designate that the projected sensing system has high sensitivity and retains a virtuous capability for various applications. To evaluate the selectivity of the sensor system, we examined the fluorescence response of the system to Zn2+ at a concentration of 40 μM with the presence of coexisting foreign substance such as Na+, K+, Li2+, Cs2+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Sr2+, Mg2+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Ag+ and Al3+ under the same conditions (Fig. 4C). It was observed that coexistence of these environmentally relevant metal ions has negligible interfering effect on Zn2+ sensing when present in the concentration of 40 μM. That is to say the established strategy to detect Zn2+ions possesses a high selectivity and specificity. Additionally, the response of the proposed sensing system as a function of time was evaluated by monitoring the alterations in the fluorescence spectrum as shown in Fig. 4D. Experimentally, CDs were subjected to different concentration of Zn2+ ions (0–40 μM) taken in different volumetric flasks and the emission spectra were noted at regular intervals. As demonstrated in Fig. 4D, Zn2+ ions bind with the CDs within first 60 s of the reaction time and no further changes in the fluorescence profile were observed thereafter up to 500 s. Further, in order to investigate the effect of pH on the response of the proposed sensor towards Zn2+ ions, fluorescence emission profile was monitored in the presence Zn2+ ions at varying pH and it was shown to exhibit stability in a wide range of pH from 5 to 13 (Fig. 3C).

Additionally, the photophysical properties of the bare CDs have also been investigated. For this, the CDs were excited at increasing wavelength from 400 to 550 nm and emission profile was scrutinized as shown in Fig. S4. It could be evidently perceived that the CDs exhibit excitation wavelength-dependent fluorescence emission spectra. Also, the metal binding ability of the CDs was also investigated in same experimental conditions. For this, 40 μM of nitrate salts of an array of metal ions viz. Li2+, Na+, K+, Cs2+, Ba2+, Sr2+,Ca2+, Mg2+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Co2+,Ni2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Ag+ and Al3+were added to the solution of CDs and fluorescence emission spectra was noted as shown in Fig. S5. None of the tested metal ions displayed significant change in emission intensity indicating that the CDs could not bind any of the metal ions. Hence, this could be concluded that the sensing properties of the functionalized CDs are due to the presence of an appropriate cavity bearing suitable binding sites viz. imine and hydroxyl groups and not due to any inherent property of the CDs itself (see Table 1 ).

Further, the sensor assembly was embedded into a solution of polyethylene maleic anhydride in tetrahydrofuran (THF) casted in a flat-bottom glass petri plate (non-fluorescent) and was set to evaporate completely. The thin film thus obtained was then subjected to 40 μM solution of Zn2+ ions and was then observed under UV light (365 nm) as shown in Fig. 3D. The variation in the fluorescence upon exposure of the film to solution containing Zn2+ ions is depicted in Fig. 3D.

The practical applications of functionalized CDs for cell imaging, in vitro cellular uptake experiments have been investigated using fluorescence imaging of E. coli cell lines. The cell line was provided by IMTECH Chandigarh India. The E. coli cells were grown in normal broth medium for 24 h incubation period. The cells were placed on a glass covers lips and allowed to adhere for at least 24 h. Further, fluorescence imaging was performed on Leica Fluorescence Microscope. For this, the cells were treated with Zn2+ ions (40 μM) dissolved in sterilized PBS (pH 7.4) and incubated for half an hour at 37 °C. The cells were then given washing with PBS thrice in order to remove any excess of the metal ions. Thereafter, culture media was added to the cell culture treated with CDs and was then incubated again for half an hour at 37 °C. After removing the culture media, the cells (treated with CDs) were washed with PBS thrice and then fixed in formaldehyde solution for 24 h of incubation. After fixation the cell was washed with PBS buffer thrice and investigating with fluorescence microscope. The cells were then excited at 330 nm using a lamp (UV) and emission filter was 418 nm.

It is quite evident that after being labeled with functionalized CDs, all cells showed light blue emissions (Fig. 5 A), whereas no fluorescence emission is detected in the control experiment indicating that the functionalized CDs have been successfully taken-up by the E. coli cells. Further, when the cells pre-treated with CDs were subjected to Zn2+ ions, a change in the fluorescence emission was observed (Fig. 5C). Hence, the CDs thus synthesized in the presence of Zn2+ ions can potentially serve as a substitute of organic dyes which are susceptible to photo-bleaching or are known to have toxic effects.

The Zn2+ ions in tap water, river water and aqueous industrial waste samples were determined using our proposed sensor. The sample treated with dilute HNO3 and 30% H2O2 was irradiated with high-pressure mercury lamp for at least 2–3 h to eliminate interference due to organic compounds. The sample was then stirred and the volume was reduced and further, it was filtered using a 0.45 μm membrane filter paper. pH was then adjusted using dilute HNO3 and Zn2+ ions were then determined using the sensor. For the determination of Zn2+ ions in zinc supplements and the Oral Rehydration Solution (ORS) available in market, the tablet and the content of the packet was first dissolved in 50 ml water and diluted 1000 times before use and pH was adjusted using dilute HNO3. The analysis was then performed on fluorescence spectrophotometer using CDs based sensor and the obtained results as presented (Table S1) were found to be comparable with the results obtained using standard methods (Atomic Absorption Spectrometry). The obtained results showed that the proposed sensor could successfully recover Zn2+ ions from various samples containing different amount of Zn2+ ions selectively with high percentage recovery.

To concise, we have developed an ingenuous approach to detect Zn2+ ions with high selectivity by calix[4]arene-modified CDs as an efficient fluorescence probe. The as-prepared CDs can be excited at different wavelengths, thereby emitting at a wide range of wavelength in the fluorescence emission spectrum. The surface passivation of the CDs obtained from hydrothermal treatment of glycerol with ethylene diamine tune the emission spectrum and emission efficiency as well as fluorescence lifetime is improved extensively. In addition to this, two forms of CDs (bare CDs and surface functionalized CDs) were shown to display stability in a wide range of pH and demonstrate excellent fluorescent properties and resistance to photo-bleaching when given UV irradiation at 365 nm at neutral pH. The toxicity and bio-imaging experiments performed on E. coli cell lines presented the as-synthesized CDs to have less cellular toxicity and outstanding bio-imaging capability. The fluorescent probe was also shown to display resistance to photo-bleaching when given UV irradiation at 365 nm. Noticeably, this work proposes the surface functionalized CDs as a promising fluorescent probe for the fabrication of fluorescent nanosensor for Zn2+ ions. The inexpensive Zn2+ sensor displays several advantages in terms of prompt detection, virtuous selectivity with low detection limit in nanomolar range, high sensitivity and wide linear response range.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Research grants from DST-INSPIRE and CSIR Project 02(0216)/14/EMR-II is highly acknowledged by the authors.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the supplementary data related to this article:
supporting information supporting information

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2017.12.048.
